lesson.title: Python errors: meet the traceback
masteryOutcomes: Understand why Python provides a traceback and how it helps identify what went wrong.
Identify parts of a traceback and different types of tracebacks.

concept: Reading a Python traceback to find what went wrong and where
prerequisites: Writing and running simple Python scripts with print()
Collecting user input with input() and converting it with int()

explanations.text: When your program breaks, Python doesn't just say 'error' — it leaves you a note explaining what went wrong and exactly which line caused it. That note is called a traceback. Learning to read it is like learning to read a treasure map: the last line tells you WHAT happened, and the lines above tell you WHERE.

A traceback is Python's error report. Read it bottom-up: the last line names the error type and gives a message, like NameError: name 'nmae' is not defined. Above that, Python shows the file and line number where the error happened, plus the offending line of code. Different problems produce different types: NameError (unknown name), TypeError (wrong kind of value), ValueError (right kind, bad content — like int("hello")), and ZeroDivisionError (dividing by zero). Try print(greeting) in the editor without defining greeting and press Run — you'll see a real NameError traceback.

When Python hits a runtime error it raises an exception, and if nothing handles it, execution stops and Python prints the traceback: the chain of calls that led to the failure, most recent call last. Each frame shows a location (line number and code), and the final line is the exception class plus its message. SyntaxError is special — it's caught before your program even runs, and Python points a caret (^) at the spot in the line where parsing failed. Runtime errors like TypeError (e.g. "hello" + 5) or ValueError (e.g. int("abc")) only appear when that line actually executes. The error type narrows down the category of problem; the message and line number pinpoint it.

examples: print(greeting)  # NameError: name 'greeting' is not defined
int("hello")  # ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'hello'
print("age: " + 5)  # TypeError: can only concatenate str (not "int") to str

hintLadder.text: Start at the bottom of the traceback — what is the very last line telling you?
The last line has two parts: the error type before the colon, and a message after it. What type do you see?
Now look one section up for the line number Python points at. Read that exact line of your code — what on it matches the error message?

misconceptions.trigger: Reads the traceback top-down and gets lost in the first lines, ignoring the final line
misconceptions.correction: Tracebacks are read bottom-up. The last line names the error type and message — that's the summary. The lines above only tell you where it happened.

misconceptions.trigger: Says 'my program has an error' without reading which error, and starts changing random lines
misconceptions.correction: The traceback already narrows it down: the type tells you the category (NameError = unknown name, ValueError = bad content, TypeError = wrong kind of value) and the line number tells you where to look. Read first, then fix one thing.

misconceptions.trigger: Thinks a traceback means Python or the computer is broken, or that they did something shameful
misconceptions.correction: A traceback is normal and helpful — every programmer sees them daily. It means Python stopped safely and wrote you a report so you can fix the exact problem.

misconceptions.trigger: Confuses ValueError with TypeError, e.g. thinks int("abc") is a TypeError
misconceptions.correction: int("abc") is a ValueError: the argument is the right kind (a string) but its content can't become a number. TypeError is for the wrong kind entirely, like "age: " + 5 mixing a string and a number.

misconceptions.trigger: Expects a SyntaxError to point at a runtime line, or is confused why the program 'didn't even start'
misconceptions.correction: SyntaxError is caught before your program runs — Python couldn't even read the code, and it points a caret (^) at the confusing spot. Runtime errors like NameError only happen while the program is running.

checks.prompt: Type print(greeting) in the editor (without defining greeting), press Run, and then tell me: what is the error type, and which line does the traceback point at?
acceptanceCriteria: Names the error type as NameError
States the line number the traceback reports (line 1 for a one-line script)
Explains the cause: the name greeting was never defined
checks.passFeedback: Exactly — you read the type from the last line and the location from the line above it. That's the whole skill.
checks.failFeedback: Look at the traceback again, starting from the bottom line. What word comes right before the colon, and what line number appears above it?

checks.prompt: Without running anything: which error type would int("ten") raise, and which would "score: " + 100 raise?
acceptanceCriteria: Distinguishes them: ValueError is right kind with bad content, TypeError is wrong kind of value
checks.passFeedback: Spot on — you can now tell the error types apart before Python even tells you.
checks.failFeedback: Think about it this way: is the value the wrong KIND (TypeError), or the right kind with content that doesn't work (ValueError)? Try running each in the editor to check.

practice.prompt: This program crashes. Press Run, read the traceback bottom-up, and fix only the line it points at so the program prints: You are 25 in five years
practice.starterCode:
age = "20"
future = age + 5
print("You are", future, "in five years")
acceptanceCriteria: Runs the starter code and identifies the TypeError on line 2
Fixes it by converting the string to a number, e.g. future = int(age) + 5
The fixed program runs without errors and prints: You are 25 in five years

practice.prompt: In your own words: why does Python give you a whole traceback instead of just stopping silently, and what are the two most useful pieces of information in it?
rubric: Says the traceback exists to help you locate and understand the problem instead of guessing
Names the error type and message (the last line) as one key piece
Names the location — line number and the code shown — as the other key piece

practice.prompt: Your traceback ends with: ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'abc'. What most likely happened?
options: You used a variable name that was never defined
You tried to convert text that isn't a number, like int("abc")
You divided a number by zero
practice.explanation: ValueError with 'invalid literal for int()' means int() received a string whose content can't become a number. An undefined name is a NameError, dividing by zero is a ZeroDivisionError, and a missing parenthesis is a SyntaxError.

pedagogyRules: Always have the student run the broken code themselves and read the real traceback aloud — never describe an error they haven't seen in their Output panel.
Teach bottom-up reading explicitly: ask 'what does the LAST line say?' before discussing anything else in the traceback.
Normalize errors immediately: frame every traceback as a helpful report, never as failure — especially if the student sounds frustrated or apologetic.
If the student says they already know how to read errors, respect it: give them the classify-errors check (ValueError vs TypeError) to verify lightly, then advance without re-drilling.

If the student jumps ahead and asks about try/except or handling errors, give a one-line preview ('that's how you catch these — it's coming soon') and steer back to reading tracebacks first, since you can't handle an error you can't identify.
When the student misreads an error, point them back to the exact words in the last line of their own traceback rather than restating the answer.
Keep the student in the editor: to settle any 'which error is this?' debate, have them type it and press Run.

failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Quote the student's actual traceback text. If the last line says ValueError, do not call it a TypeError or invent a different cause — the error type and message in the Output panel are the ground truth.

Only confirm a fix if the student re-runs the code and the traceback is gone with correct output. A changed line that still errors, or errors differently, is progress but not success — say so honestly.

If the student is lost, drop to one question at a time: first 'what error type is on the last line?', then 'what line number is shown above it?', then 'what's on that line?'. Never analyze the whole traceback for them in one go.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Student frustration or wanting to skip errors entirely
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Acknowledge the feeling, then reframe with one concrete win: 'this traceback just told you the exact line to fix — that saves you re-reading the whole program.' Offer one tiny step (read only the last line) instead of the full exercise.

chunks.text: A traceback is the error report Python prints when a program fails. It exists so you don't have to guess: it tells you WHAT went wrong (the error type and message) and WHERE (the file, line number, and the line of code). Tracebacks are read bottom-up — the last line is the summary, the lines above are the location.

The last line of a traceback has two parts separated by a colon: the error type, then the message. Example: NameError: name 'greeting' is not defined. The type gives the category of problem; the message gives the specific detail. Directly above, Python shows the location, e.g. File "<stdin>", line 1, along with the code on that line.

Common runtime error types: NameError — you used a name that was never defined, often a typo (print(nmae)). TypeError — you combined the wrong kinds of values, like "age: " + 5 (string plus number). ValueError — the kind is right but the content is bad, like int("hello"). ZeroDivisionError — you divided by zero, like 10 / 0.

SyntaxError is different from runtime errors: Python catches it BEFORE the program runs, because it couldn't even read the code. Python prints the confusing line with a caret (^) pointing at or near the problem spot, e.g. a missing closing quote or parenthesis. If you see SyntaxError, none of your program executed yet.

Worked example. Running: age = "20" then future = age + 5 produces: Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>", line 2, in <module> ... TypeError: can only concatenate str (not "int") to str. Reading bottom-up: the type is TypeError, the message says a string was added to an int, and the location line says it happened on line 2. Fix: future = int(age) + 5, then press Run again to confirm the traceback is gone.

celebration.headline: You can read Python's error reports like a pro.
celebration.recap: You learned why Python prints a traceback, how to read it bottom-up to find the error type, message, and line number, and how to tell NameError, TypeError, ValueError, ZeroDivisionError, and SyntaxError apart.
celebration.unlocked: Next: use what tracebacks tell you to debug programs step by step.

summaryBullets: You can now read a traceback bottom-up to find the error type, its message, and the exact line that failed.
You can now tell apart NameError, TypeError, ValueError, ZeroDivisionError, and SyntaxError from the last line alone.
You can now explain why a SyntaxError stops your program before it even starts, while runtime errors happen mid-run.
You can now fix a crashing program by going straight to the line the traceback points at instead of guessing.
openingLine: Hey {name} — today we're going to break some programs on purpose, and I promise it's going to be fun. Python leaves you a detailed note every time something goes wrong, called a traceback, and once you can read it, errors stop being scary. Ready to crash your first program?
bridgeFromPreviousLesson: Last time you converted user input with int() before computing — and if someone had typed 'ten' instead of 10, your program would have crashed, so today you'll learn to read exactly what Python says when that happens.

turns.text: I ran int("hello") and got an error. It's a TypeError because a string is the wrong type for int().
Good instinct to name the type — but let's check against the real report. Look at the last line of your traceback: what word comes right before the colon?
Oh... it says ValueError, not TypeError.

Exactly, and here's the difference: int() happily accepts strings, so the KIND is fine — it's the CONTENT 'hello' that can't become a number. That's ValueError. What would int() need to receive to raise it a string it likes?
Something like "42", a string that's actually a number.
Perfect. Type int("42") in the editor and press Run to prove it to yourself.

dialogueExemplars.title: Why do I even need to read errors?

turns.text: Honestly, why do I need this? Can't I just fix my code until it works?

Fair question! Imagine your input program from last lesson running for a real user who types 'ten' as their age — it crashes on one specific line. Without the traceback you'd re-read every line guessing; with it, Python hands you the line number and the exact reason. Want to see it save you time right now?
Type print(10 / 0) in the editor and press Run. Then tell me just the last line of what appears — that one line is the whole diagnosis.

dialogueExemplars.title: I already know errors — can we skip?

turns.text: I've seen plenty of error messages before, can we skip this?
If you've got it, we move on — no re-drilling, deal. Quick check first: without running anything, which error does int("ten") raise, and which does "score: " + 100 raise?
The first is a ValueError and the second is a TypeError.
That's exactly right — right kind, bad content versus wrong kind entirely. You've earned the skip; let's jump straight to the fix-the-crash exercise and put it to work.
